query: evidence on flexible instruments and femoral tunnel length at 90 degree knee flexion in anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction

retrieved_docs.title: Lateral Collateral Ligament Knee Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Isometric LCL reconstruction
retrieved_docs.content: A nonanatomic technique was described using patellar tendon allograft. The proximal fibula is cleared of all soft tissues. A tunnel is then drilled and gradually enlarged with reaming. The femur is exposed through a midline incision in the ITB, and a tunnel is drilled 6 mm anterior to the center of the LCL femoral insertion. The tunnel should be directed proximal and anterior to the medial femoral epicondyle. The surgeon can confirm isometry using a small K-wire and a looped suture.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cruciate Ligament Knee Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Tunnel Placement
retrieved_docs.content: Complications reported following hamstring harvest include residual hamstring weakness and paresthesia due to saphenous nerve branch injury, where an oblique or horizontal incision can reduce this risk. A "windshield wiper" effect has been described when suspensory fixation away from the joint line would result in tunnel abrasion and dilation with knee range of movement.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cruciate Ligament Knee Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Tunnel Placement
retrieved_docs.content: Graft placement and fixation: preconditioning of the graft can reduce up to 50% of the stress relaxation. Tensioning at 20 or 40 N has no clinical effects on outcomes. Ideally, the graft should be fixed in 20 to 30 degrees of flexion. Multiple options are available for fixation, which can be used alone or in combination. Aperture or compression fixation can be achieved with an interference screw, whereas suspensory fixation can be achieved by cortical buttons, screws, and washer post or staple fixation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cruciate Ligament Knee Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Tunnel Placement
retrieved_docs.content: Graft processing: F resh-frozen grafts have lower re-rupture rates than chemically treated or irradiated grafts. The reason is that the supercritical CO2 decreases the structural and mechanical properties of the grafts. Radiation has effects depending on its dose. More than 3 Mrads kill HIV but at the expense of reduced structural and mechanical properties of the graft. Between 2-2.8, Mrad, and 1 to 1.2, Mrad decreases stiffness by 30% and 20%, respectively— deep freezing and c hlorhexidine gluconate (4%) damage cells without affecting the graft's strength.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Equipment -- Cruciate-Retaining
retrieved_docs.content: Resemblance of more normal knee kinematics and anatomy, theoretically. Preservation of bone stock (less distal femur resected compared to posterior-stabilized TKA prosthesis). Retention of native posterior cruciate ligament proprioception. Proposed disadvantages of the cruciate-retaining TKA design include: A tight posterior cruciate ligament that leads to early or accelerated polyethylene wear. A loose or ruptured posterior cruciate ligament that results in flexion instability and possible subluxation or dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Cruciate Ligament Knee Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Tunnel Placement
retrieved_docs.content: Femur side; transtibial or tibia-independent, either inside-out or outside-in technique. Ideally, tunnel placement in the sagittal plane should be within 1-2 mm of the posterior femoral cortex. In the coronal plane, the aim is to position a more horizontal graft to reduce rotational laxity. So, using the clock face of the lateral wall, the graft should be positioned at 2 o'clock for the left knee or 10 o'clock for the right knee. Anteromedial and far medial drilling portals should be used to facilitate the proper femoral tunnel positioning. Drilling in over 70 degrees of flexion will avoid posterior wall blowout. [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Equipment -- Cruciate Retaining (CR)
retrieved_docs.content: The ACL is sacrificed; overall feasibility mandates a competent and functional PCL. It can be used in those with mild varus/valgus deformity. It should be avoided in those with inflammatory arthritis due to increased risk for short-term or delayed rupture of PCL. Benefits: Fewer patellar complications (theoretical) Increased quadriceps strength (controversial, depends in part on surgical exposure utilized) Improved stair climbing Preserved PCL proprioception (theoretical) Lower shear forces on the tibial component Improved femoral bone stock preservation Preserves near-normal knee kinematics Avoids cam-post jump complication that exists in posterior stabilized prosthetic components Disadvantages: Risk of postoperative PCL degeneration or rupture that can lead to flexion instability Tight PCL can lead to increased wear on polyethylene and dysfunctional TKA kinematics.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) Important for axial rotation as well as preventing anterior translation of the tibia on the femur Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) Important to prevent posterior translation of the tibia on the femur and allow for femoral rollback. Lateral collateral ligament (LCL) Varus restraint and is typically attenuated in significant varus deformity Medial collateral ligament (MCL) Valgus restraint and is typically attenuated in significant valgus deformity. Posterolateral corner (PLC) The primary stabilizer of external tibial rotation Mechanical Alignment [3] The distal femur is in approximately 9 degrees of anatomic valgus relative to the joint line, while the proximal tibia in 3 degrees of anatomic varus relative to the joint line. The typical patellar Q angle is between 13 degrees and 19 degrees, with an increased Q angle increases the risk of patellar maltracking and dislocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- Procedural Steps
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the flexion gap is attained by using an anteroposterior sizing guide positioned with respect to the bony landmarks on the femur (usually the Whiteside line or the native transepicondylar axis). Depending on the surgeon's preference for anterior or posterior referencing, the flexion gap is established and fine-tuned using system-specific incremental sizing adjustments available with the cutting guides. In addition, it is essential to visualize the flexion gap before making the bony cuts and to ensure proper soft tissue balancing.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bony Pelvis and Lower Limb: Knee Posterior Cruciate Ligament -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Orthopedic surgeons use two methods to place the graft, with the goal being to recreate the optimal biomechanical restraint of the PCL. The tunnel method is an arthroscopic procedure in which graft placement is in an acute angular turn. The placement of the graft in this method can potentially elongate and thin the graft over time. The tibial inlay approach places the graft near the trough at the tibial insertion of the PCL and avoids having to place the graft in the acute angular turn used in the tunnel method; however, the tibial inlay approach is an open procedure. Additional research may determine if there are better functional, long-term outcomes for one approach over the other. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Arthroplasty Techniques -- Equipment -- Posterior-Stabilized
retrieved_docs.content: The posterior-stabilized TKA design is slightly more constrained and requires the surgeon to sacrifice the posterior cruciate ligament. The femoral component contains a cam designed to engage with the tibial polyethylene post during knee flexion. Proposed advantages of the posterior-stabilized TKA design include: Overall knee balancing in the setting of an absent posterior cruciate ligament. Better knee flexion, theoretically. Lower ranges of axial rotation and condylar translation Proposed disadvantages of the posterior-stabilized TKA design include: Potential cam jump due to a loose flexion gap or knee hyperextension. Risk of patellar clunk syndrome. Possibility of tibial post-wear or fracture.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Each implant company has a specific system with trays that come with the necessary tools and trial components. The basic equipment that will be used in all cases and will improve the efficiency and safety of the case include: Standard operating table De Mayo leg holder, paint roller, or other equivalent patient positioning instrumentation Tourniquet Overall use remains debated in the literature Use and duration is surgeon-specific Retractors Osteotomes Sagittal saw Mallet Lamina spreaders Cement-specific equipment Cement mixer system Pressurizing gun Pulse lavage irrigation Suction Bovie electrocautery In revision cases, may also require other equipment, including: Curettes Reamers Cones Sleeves Stemmed options including offset adjusting capabilities Bone grafting options

retrieved_docs.title: Knee Arthroplasty(Archived) -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: A TKA system will consist of instrumentation that helps the surgeon prepare the ends of the femur, tibia, and patella to receive an implant.  The instrumentation will be specific to the brand and type of implant being used with each company and model having specific intricacies. In general, the instrumentation will consist of: Intramedullary femoral guide to help establish the distal femoral alignment Distal femoral cutting guide Femoral sizing guide 4-in-1 femoral cutting guide Extramedullary or intramedullary tibial guide Proximal tibial cutting guide Patella sizing guide Femoral component trial Tibial baseplate trial Patellar button trial Trial plastic bearing The final implants will come in individual sterile packages and will consist of: Femoral component, typically made of cobalt-chrome Tibial component, typically made of cobalt-chrome or titanium Tibial polyethylene bearing, made of an ultra high molecular weight (UHMW) polyethylene Patellar button, made of UHMW polyethylene

retrieved_docs.content: A good understanding of the anatomy of attachments, the direction of the two bundles of ACL, and its status during flexion and extension of the knee is vital in achieving correct tunnel placement during surgery for securing the graft and obtain good tensioning of the graft.

retrieved_docs.title: Popliteal Aneurysm Repair(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment -- A doppler is brought onto the field, and flow is checked proximal and distal to the anastomosis and in the foot. Some providers open the aneurysm sac from above and below the knee and tie off any patent branch vessels present to prevent persistent flow into the sac despite exclusion. The wounds are irrigated and closed in multiple layers. Open Repair, Posterior Approach
retrieved_docs.content: An S-shaped incision starts on the thigh's medial side, extends along the knee's flexion crease, and ends on the lateral aspect of the calf. The small saphenous vein is examined and harvested if it is of adequate size. If not, the greater saphenous vein may be harvested from the thigh with the patient prone. This is marked for later reversal and stored in heparinized saline. Injury to the medial sural nerve should be avoided. The fascia is incised just medial to the small saphenous vein. The popliteal artery and vein are deep and medial to the tibial nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Knee Replacement Techniques -- Technique or Treatment -- 1) Measured Resection
retrieved_docs.content: The pitfall of the technique is that it is difficult to perform soft-tissue releases after performing bony cuts. Ligament releases after bony cuts can affect the flexion/extension gaps and lead to poor balancing. Changing ligament tension in extension will affect the flexion gap and vice versa.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: A wide range of orthopedic interventions are linked to the femur, reflecting the bone’s central role in lower‑extremity surgery. The procedure described as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula” is associated with the femur, indicating that epiphyseal arrest techniques may involve the distal femur in conjunction with the proximal tibia and fibula. Direct surgical actions on the femur include “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” and “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” each of which specifies the femur as the direct procedure site. Procedures that are performed on or modify the femur more generally are identified as having the femur as the procedure site, such as “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Interventions that involve the femur in a less direct manner are classified with an indirect relationship, exemplified by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Suture of periosteum of femur,” both of which denote the femur as an indirect procedure site. Together, these relationships delineate how various surgical techniques—ranging from fracture fixation and bone shortening to prosthetic revision and soft‑tissue repair—are organized around the femur, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding femoral‑focused orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: The procedural category **THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS** encompasses a comprehensive set of operative interventions that address fractures, dislocations, deformities, limb‑length discrepancies, prosthetic failures, and reconstructive needs of the hip, femur, tibia, fibula, and adjacent lower‑extremity structures. Direct‑site femoral procedures include **internal fixation of femur** (with or without fracture reduction), **intramedullary nailing of femur**, **open reduction of fracture of femur** (with or without internal fixation), **femoral shortening with blade plate**, **repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck**, and various **osteoplasties of femur** (plain, lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with femoral segment transfer). **Trochanterplasty** and **femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ)** are also performed directly on the femur. Growth‑plate modulation techniques such as **arrest, epiphyseal, any method

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 989
retrieved_docs.content: THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS is classified as a subcategory of treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur). Within this category, a range of specific operative interventions are enumerated, including Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., genu varus or valgus); internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction; open reduction of fracture of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation; osteoplasty of femur; osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer; repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck; revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure); and trochanterplasty. These procedures address various deformities, fractures, malunions, and prosthetic revisions of the thigh and leg, providing surgical options for correction, stabilization, and reconstruction. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical interventions within the broader context of lower‑extremity trauma and orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 979
retrieved_docs.content: The category THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS, which is classified under the broader treatment of fracture or dislocation of the lower extremity (other than hip or femur), comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions. These interventions include arrest, epiphyseal, any method (e.g., epiphysiodesis) of the distal femur; arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., for genu varus or valgus); femoral shortening with blade plate; internal fixation of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur; osteoplasty of femur, lengthening; prophylactic treatment of femur; replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate; and revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure). Together, these procedures represent the surgical repertoire for addressing complex thigh and leg injuries, deformities, and prosthetic revisions, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate reparative and reconstructive strategies for optimal patient care.

Procedures that modify femoral length or geometry are classified as having a procedural site rather than a direct site and comprise osteoplasty of the femur for lengthening, osteoplasty of the femur for shortening, and combined lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer, which are employed to correct limb‑length discrepancy and angular deformities. Indirect femoral interventions include replacement of a femoral prosthesis using methyl methacrylate, revision of total knee arthroplasty (all components), and prophylactic treatment of the femur; these are performed as part of broader reconstructive or prosthetic strategies rather than primary bone fixation. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 164
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category aggregates a wide array of operative interventions that target the femur, tibia, fibula, hip, and associated joint structures. Direct femoral procedures include internal fixation of the femur (with or without fracture reduction), intramedullary nailing, open reduction of femur fractures (with or without internal fixation), suture of the periosteum of the femur, repair of distal femur malunion, femoral‑head reconstruction (إعاضة رأس الفخذ), and femoral shortening performed with a blade plate. Osteoplastic techniques—osteoplasty of the femur, femoral lengthening, shortening, and combined lengthening‑shortening with segment transfer—address limb‑length discrepancies and deformities. Growth‑modulating strategies comprise epiphysiodesis of the distal femur (and proximal tibia or fibula) and hemiepiphyseal arrest of the distal femur, proximal tibia, or fibula for correction of genu varus or valgus.
